stem: A factory's output comes from two machines. Machine A makes \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[t]]} \) of all items and Machine B makes the rest. An item from Machine A is defective with probability \( \frac{[[dan]]}{10} \), and an item from Machine B is defective with probability \( \frac{[[dbn]]}{10} \). An item is selected at random and found to be defective. What is the probability it was made by Machine A?
solution: By Bayes' theorem, \( P(A\mid D) = \frac{P(A)P(D\mid A)}{P(A)P(D\mid A) + P(B)P(D\mid B)} \). The denominator is \( P(D) \) from the law of total probability, giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the prior \( P(A) \), ignoring the defective evidence.
This is the likelihood \( P(D\mid A) \), not the posterior \( P(A\mid D) \).
This is the joint probability \( P(A \cap D) \); it was not divided by \( P(D) \).
This is \( P(B\mid D) \), the probability the item came from the other machine.
This uses only the defect rates and ignores the machines' production shares (priors).
This is \( P(D) \), the overall defect probability (the denominator), not the posterior.

stem: A warehouse stocks bulbs from two suppliers. Supplier X provides \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[t]]} \) of the bulbs and Supplier Y provides the rest. \( \frac{[[dan]]}{10} \) of Supplier X's bulbs are defective, while \( \frac{[[dbn]]}{10} \) of Supplier Y's bulbs are defective. If a bulb is chosen at random from the warehouse's full stock, what is the (overall) probability that it is defective?
solution: By the law of total probability, \( P(\text{defective}) = P(X)P(\text{def}\mid X) + P(Y)P(\text{def}\mid Y) = \frac{[[a]]}{[[t]]}\cdot\frac{[[dan]]}{10} + \frac{[[t]]-[[a]]}{[[t]]}\cdot\frac{[[dbn]]}{10} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This simply averages the two defect rates, ignoring the suppliers' unequal market shares.
This gives only Supplier X's contribution to the total, dropping Supplier Y's term entirely.
This gives only Supplier Y's contribution to the total, dropping Supplier X's term entirely.
This multiplies the two defect rates together instead of weighting them by supplier share and summing.
This adds a share and a defect rate directly, mixing incompatible quantities.
This adds the numerators and denominators of the two weighted terms separately instead of computing a weighted sum.

stem: A part is supplied by three plants, Plant 1, Plant 2, and Plant 3, which produce \( \frac{[[a1]]}{[[t]]} \), \( \frac{[[a2]]}{[[t]]} \), and \( \frac{[[a3]]}{[[t]]} \) of all parts respectively. The probability a part is defective is \( \frac{[[d1]]}{10} \), \( \frac{[[d2]]}{10} \), and \( \frac{[[d3]]}{10} \) for parts from Plant 1, Plant 2, and Plant 3 respectively. A part is chosen at random and found to be defective. What is the probability it came from Plant 2?

answer_spec.expr: (Rational([[a2]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d2]], 10)) / (Rational([[a1]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d1]], 10) + Rational([[a2]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d2]], 10) + Rational([[a3]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d3]], 10))

distractors: (Rational([[a1]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d1]], 10)) / (Rational([[a1]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d1]], 10) + Rational([[a2]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d2]], 10) + Rational([[a3]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d3]], 10))
(Rational([[a3]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d3]], 10)) / (Rational([[a1]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d1]], 10) + Rational([[a2]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d2]], 10) + Rational([[a3]], [[t]]) * Rational([[d3]], 10))
solution: By Bayes' theorem with three sources, \( P(\text{Plant 2}\mid D) = \frac{P(\text{Plant 2})P(D\mid\text{Plant 2})}{\sum_{i=1}^{3} P(\text{Plant }i)P(D\mid\text{Plant }i)} = [[answer]] \), where the denominator is the law of total probability over all three plants.
distractor_rationales: This is just the prior share of Plant 2, ignoring the defect evidence entirely.
This is just Plant 2's defect rate, not the posterior probability the part came from Plant 2.
This is \( P(\text{Plant 1}\mid D) \), the posterior for a different plant.
This is \( P(\text{Plant 3}\mid D) \), the posterior for a different plant.
This is the joint probability \( P(\text{Plant 2} \cap D) \); it was never divided by the total probability of a defect.
This omits Plant 3's contribution entirely from the law-of-total-probability denominator.

stem: For events \( A \) and \( B \): \( P(A) = \frac{[[a]]}{[[t]]} \), \( P(B \mid A) = \frac{[[p1n]]}{[[p1d]]} \), and \( P(B \mid A^c) = \frac{[[p2n]]}{[[p2d]]} \), where \( A^c \) is the complement of \( A \). What is \( P(A \mid B) \)?
solution: By Bayes' theorem, \( P(A\mid B) = \dfrac{P(A)P(B\mid A)}{P(A)P(B\mid A) + P(A^c)P(B\mid A^c)} = [[answer]] \), using the law of total probability to expand \( P(B) \) in the denominator.
distractor_rationales: This is just the prior \( P(A) \), ignoring event \( B \) occurring.
This is just the likelihood \( P(B\mid A) \), not the posterior \( P(A\mid B) \).
This is the joint probability \( P(A \cap B) \), never divided by \( P(B) \).
This is \( P(A^c \mid B) \), the posterior of the complementary event.
This ignores the prior weights entirely and just compares the two conditional probabilities directly.
This is \( P(B) \), the total probability of \( B \) (the denominator), not the posterior \( P(A \mid B) \).
